steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researcher claims the ten-year-old survey no longer reflects today's residents' feelings. To support this, we need evidence that something relevant to walking behavior has changed in the neighborhood over the past ten years. The passage names sidewalks, crosswalks, and weather as the key factors influencing how far people will walk.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "The number of sidewalks and crosswalks in the neighborhood that the streetcar stop will service has increased substantially in the last ten years.": The infrastructure factors the passage links directly to walking behavior have changed in this neighborhood since the survey was taken — exactly the evidence needed to show the old data is outdated. ✓
  • "Residents of Detroit are much less likely to use public transit on rainy days than on clear days.": Describes an existing condition rather than a shift over the past ten years, so it doesn't prove the old survey is now inaccurate. ✗
  • "There has been a sharp increase in the last ten years in cyclists who use the roads in the neighborhood that the streetcar stop will service.": Addresses something the passage does not identify as a factor influencing walking behavior. ✗
  • "Current users of the Jefferson Avenue Streetcar are satisfied with the number of stops along the line.": Resident satisfaction with something other than walkability doesn't bear on whether the survey is outdated. ✗
That matches the correct answer — A is correct.
